mn3:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn3:2.1: “Be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], *bhikkhus*, not my heirs in material things.
mn3:2.10: And I too would not be criticized because of that:
mn3:2.11: ‘The teacher’s disciples dwell as heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not as heirs in material things.’
mn3:2.12: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not my heirs in material things.
mn3:2.14: ‘How might my disciples be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not my heirs in material things?’
mn3:2.3: ‘How might my disciples be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not my heirs in material things?’
mn3:2.4: And if you were my heirs in material things, *bhikkhus*, not my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], you too would be criticized because of that:
mn3:2.5: ‘The teacher’s disciples dwell as heirs in material things, not as heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*].’
mn3:2.6: And I too would be criticized because of that:
mn3:2.7: ‘The teacher’s disciples dwell as heirs in material things, not as heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*].’
mn3:2.8: And if you were my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], *bhikkhus*, not my heirs in material things, you too would not be criticized because of that:
mn3:2.9: ‘The teacher’s disciples dwell as heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not as heirs in material things.’
mn3:3.1: Here, *bhikkhus*, suppose I had eaten, declined further offerings, become full, finished, satisfied as much as needed;
mn3:3.10: and this almsfood of the *Bhagavā* is extra, fit to be thrown away.
mn3:3.11: If we do not eat it, the *Bhagavā* will now throw it away where there is little greenery, or sink it in water without living creatures.
mn3:3.12: But this was said by the *Bhagavā*:
mn3:3.13: “Be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], *bhikkhus*, not my heirs in material things.”
mn3:3.14: And this almsfood, after all, is certainly a material thing.
mn3:3.15: What if, without eating this almsfood, I were to pass this day and night with this very hunger and weakness?’
mn3:3.16: Without eating that almsfood, he would pass that day and night with that very hunger and weakness.
mn3:3.18: ‘The *Bhagavā* has eaten, declined further offerings, become full, finished, satisfied as much as needed;
mn3:3.19: and this almsfood of the *Bhagavā* is extra, fit to be thrown away.
mn3:3.2: and there were for me almsfood that was extra, fit to be thrown away.
mn3:3.20: If we do not eat it, the *Bhagavā* will now throw it away where there is little greenery, or sink it in water without living creatures.
mn3:3.21: What if I were to eat this almsfood, dispel hunger and weakness, and thus pass this day and night?’
mn3:3.22: Having eaten that almsfood and dispelled hunger and weakness, he would pass that day and night.
mn3:3.23: Even though that *bhikkhu*, *bhikkhus*, having eaten that almsfood and dispelled hunger and weakness, would pass that day and night thus, still that former *bhikkhu* is more honored and more praised by me.
mn3:3.25: Because that will lead, for that *bhikkhu*, for a long time, to wanting little, contentment, effacement, being easy to support, and the arousing of energy.
mn3:3.26: Therefore, *bhikkhus*, be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not my heirs in material things.
mn3:3.28: ‘How might my disciples be my heirs in *dhamma* [*dhammadāyādā*], not my heirs in material things?’”
mn3:3.3: Then two *bhikkhus* might come, afflicted by hunger and weakness.
mn3:3.5: ‘*bhikkhus*, I have eaten, declined further offerings, become full, finished, satisfied as much as needed;
mn3:3.6: and I have this almsfood, extra, fit to be thrown away.
mn3:3.7: If you wish, eat it. But if you do not eat it, I will now throw it away where there is little greenery, or sink it in water without living creatures.’
mn3:3.9: ‘The *Bhagavā* has eaten, declined further offerings, become full, finished, satisfied as much as needed;
mn3:4.2: Having said this, the Fortunate One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling.
mn3:4.3: Then, not long after the *Bhagavā* had left, Venerable Sāriputta addressed the *bhikkhus*:
mn3:5.1: “To what extent, friends, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], do the disciples not train in seclusion [*viveka*], and to what extent, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], do the disciples train in seclusion [*viveka*]?”
mn3:5.2: “Even from far away, friend, we come to the presence of Venerable Sāriputta to understand the meaning of this statement.
mn3:5.3: May the meaning of this statement occur to Venerable Sāriputta himself.
mn3:5.4: Having heard it from Venerable Sāriputta, the *bhikkhus* will remember it.”
mn3:5.5: “Then, friends, listen and pay careful attention; I will speak.”
mn3:5.6: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to Venerable Sāriputta.
mn3:6.1: “To what extent, friends, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], do the disciples not train in seclusion [*viveka*]?
mn3:6.10: ‘They are indulgent, lax, taking the lead in backsliding, having laid down the burden when it comes to seclusion [*paviveke*]’—
mn3:6.11: by this third respect elder *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:6.12: Elder *bhikkhus*, friends, are blameworthy in these three respects.
mn3:6.13: There, friends, middle-ranking *bhikkhus* … new *bhikkhus* are blameworthy in three respects.
mn3:6.14: New *bhikkhus* are blameworthy in three respects.
mn3:6.15: ‘While the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], the disciples do not train in seclusion [*viveka*]’—
mn3:6.16: by this first respect new *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:6.17: ‘The teacher speaks of abandoning certain *dhammas*, and they do not abandon those *dhammas*’—
mn3:6.18: by this second respect new *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:6.19: ‘They are indulgent, lax, taking the lead in backsliding, having laid down the burden when it comes to seclusion [*paviveke*]’—
mn3:6.2: Here, friends, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], the disciples do not train in seclusion [*viveka*]:
mn3:6.20: by this third respect new *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:6.21: New *bhikkhus*, friends, are blameworthy in these three respects.
mn3:6.22: To this extent, friends, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], the disciples do not train in seclusion [*viveka*].
mn3:6.3: the teacher speaks of abandoning certain *dhammas*, and they do not abandon those *dhammas*;
mn3:6.4: they are indulgent, lax, taking the lead in backsliding, having laid down the burden when it comes to seclusion [*paviveke*].
mn3:6.5: There, friends, elder *bhikkhus* are blameworthy in three respects.
mn3:6.6: ‘While the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], the disciples do not train in seclusion [*viveka*]’—
mn3:6.7: by this first respect elder *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:6.8: ‘The teacher speaks of abandoning certain *dhammas*, and they do not abandon those *dhammas*’—
mn3:6.9: by this second respect elder *bhikkhus* are blameworthy.
mn3:7.1: And to what extent, friends, while the teacher dwells secluded [*pavivittassa*], do the disciples train in seclusion [*viveka*]?
mn3:7.10: ‘They are not indulgent, not lax; they have laid down the burden of backsliding and take the lead in seclusion [*paviveke*]’—
mn3:7.11: by this third respect elder *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy.
mn3:7.12: Elder *bhikkhus*, friends, are praiseworthy in these three respects.
mn3:7.13: There, friends, middle-ranking *bhikkhus* … new *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy in three respects.

mn3:7.16: by this first respect new *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy.

mn3:7.18: by this second respect new *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy.

mn3:7.20: by this third respect new *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy.

mn3:7.5: There, friends, elder *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy in three respects.

mn3:7.9: by this second respect elder *bhikkhus* are praiseworthy.
mn3:8.1: There, friends, greed is bad and hatred is bad.
mn3:8.2: For the abandoning of greed and the abandoning of hatred there is a middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*.
mn3:8.3: And what, friends, is that middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*?
mn3:8.4: It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
mn3:8.5: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
mn3:8.6: This, friends, is that middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*.
mn3:9-15.1: There, friends, anger is bad and resentment is bad …
mn3:9-15.10: It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
mn3:9-15.11: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
mn3:9-15.12: This, friends, is that middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*.”
mn3:9-15.13: This is what Venerable Sāriputta said.
mn3:9-15.14: Those *bhikkhus* were glad and delighted in Venerable Sāriputta’s words.
mn3:9-15.2: denigration is bad and rivalry is bad,
mn3:9-15.3: jealousy is bad and stinginess is bad,
mn3:9-15.4: deceit is bad and craftiness is bad,
mn3:9-15.5: stubbornness is bad and aggression is bad,
mn3:9-15.6: conceit is bad and arrogance is bad,
mn3:9-15.7: intoxication is bad and heedlessness is bad.
mn3:9-15.8: For the abandoning of intoxication and the abandoning of heedlessness there is a middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*.
mn3:9-15.9: And what, friends, is that middle way that gives rise to vision and gives rise to knowledge, leading to peace, direct knowledge, awakening, and *nibbāna*?
